verdicts.note: Borderline toward 0: dossier notes she 'emphasizes stewardship and transparency of the county's roughly $20 billion biennial budget' [S1][S2], but the dominant thrust is expansion ('massively expand investment,' 'deeper investment in mental health and addiction services'), so +1 medium stands.

Checked +2 magnitude: platform is fully pole_b — 'prevention over enforcement,' expanded diversion, and 'restorative pathways rather than incarceration' [S1][S2], with no enforcement-side planks. Medium is correct since this is platform rhetoric, not a record.

Checked whether seven years as a county employee undercuts the +2 outsider score; it doesn't — the axis measures elected/insider tenure, and she is the only candidate with no elected record, explicitly running on lived experience [S1][S2]. The basis honestly names the county-staff angle.

Borderline on +2 magnitude: the direct tax evidence is one pamphlet line ('progressive funding options so the ultra-wealthy pay their share' [S1]), but backing a new county property-tax levy now [S4] is a second, distinct pole_b commitment, so +2 medium is defensible.
Airtight: levy-now stance, community land trusts on county land, public bank, and I-5 lid proposal are repeated across S1, S3, and S4; high confidence is earned.
Dossier flags the OLEO police-accountability work as 'campaign characterization; no independent detail found' [S1]; +1 medium correctly stays modest rather than leaning on it.

-1 rather than -2 is right: elected only since 2022, though with a real executive record; the dossier frames her as the executive-experience contrast to Saldaña's decade-long legislative record [S3][S4].

Direction and confidence hold (repeated statements across S1/S2/S4 plus co-sponsorship), and her levy reticence is pole_b reasoning (preferring more-progressive revenue, not opposing taxes). One flag for the highlights text: 'championed the capital gains tax' is the pamphlet's campaign characterization — the dossier notes her own site describes her as a co-sponsor, and the record section calls these 'campaign characterizations of shared legislative efforts.'

Borderline high confidence: evidence is a platform line ('affordable at any income' [S1]), the forum funding stance [S4], and her Puget Sound Sage affordable-housing background — repeated across sources, so high squeaks by; the conservative +1 already accounts for the levy hesitancy.
Single pamphlet statement ('will expand behavioral health and crisis response' [S1]) correctly capped at +1 medium.

Confidence overstated. The verified record (S3, official bio) is HEAL Act prime-sponsorship — an environmental-justice law that only partially addresses this axis's carbon-pricing-vs-affordability tension. The carbon-pricing evidence (Climate Commitment Act, Clean Fuel Standard) is explicitly labeled in the dossier as 'campaign characterizations of shared legislative efforts' [S1][S2], not prime-sponsored record. Direction is unambiguous and +2 defensible, but this is a single verified record item plus self-characterizations: medium, not high.
-2 high is airtight: a decade in the senate, Labor & Commerce chair, multiple prime-sponsored passed laws [S3] — the dossier calls it 'the deepest legislative paper trail in this race.'
